title: Lec-40: Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) in Computer Networks

segments.text: Dear students, welcome to Gate Smashers. In today's video I'm going to explain Token Ring
or we call it IEEE 802.5 standard or protocol.
Actually it was originally designed by IBM in 1984
but later it became standard and it is defined in IEEE 802.5
so all important points regarding this I will tell you in this video because
in competitive exams or your college/University exams small point on this can be asked.
Each and every point you can understand from here.
So guys quickly like the video, subscribe the channel if you haven't yet
and if you have then you can subscribe from more devices.
Subscribers are very important.
So let's start, first of all I'll tell you as the name suggests, Ring, Token ring.
So this uses which topology? It uses Ring topology.
Why this is important actually? Because in IEEE 802.3 we discussed ethernet.
In that bus topology is used and if we talk about Data Link layer,
the standard protocols in Data Link layer like ethernet, IEEE 802.3 or 802.5,
they ask what are the differences, which topology it uses,
so you should know these points.
So Ring Topology is used here and second point here is
which access control method we use? Token Passing. What does this mean?
See, here if we talk about ring topology or if we talk about Token Ring,
how will you make its diagram, these are different stations
and how these stations are connected this way through Ring topology.
There are multiple stations, give names if you want to like A, B, C and D.
Now look, here A, B, C and D are all connected by ring topology.
But which device will do transmission when,
and which device will not do transmission now, how will we know about this?
Because if all do transmission at a time, then there will be collision.
So which device will access first, who will access media first, how to decide this,
for this we use concept of token passing.
So what happens here, a token is revolving, means it is moving.
So the station which is ready with data will capture the token, hold it,
and then start transmission of data.
And when its transmission will be complete, it will release the token.
This is a simple concept. Then similarly, the token will again keep revolving.
Then the station which is ready will capture the token before sending the data,
and then start transmitting data.
No one else can send data at this time because it doesn't have the token.
So who has the token can only transmit the data. This simple concept we use here.
So token ring, next point comes whether it is bidirectional or unidirectional,
so it is unidirectional, means you can use it in simplex mode
whether you take clockwise or anticlockwise, but movement will be in 1 direction only,
data will move only in 1 direction.
If we talk about data rate, then the data rate we use here is 4Mbps and 16Mbps.
Earlier these were used, now slowly many variations came in this,
so multiple can come, but if you are asked by default then you can say
4Mbps and 16Mbps. What is data rate? Which we call bandwidth also,
how many bits of data we can transmit per second, that is data rate.
And if we talk about token, token also has to be released.
Like you captured the token, started data transmission,
you transmitted the data, sent it. Now you will release the token.
So there are 2 concepts of releasing the token,
early token release or delayed token release. Early means that
as soon as you sent all packets, all messages, you release the token immediately.
And if we talk about delay, what happens in delay is you transmitted the packet first,
then second packet, when that packet goes around and returns to you,
then you will know that your packet has reached the whole ring.
Now you will release the token. This means delayed.
There are some numericals on this, but they usually come in GATE or UGC-NET exams
but in normal exams they are not asked.
But still in the next video we will do a discussion on that.
Then if we talk about acknowledgement,
concept of acknowledgement is not mentioned clear-cut anywhere.
Whether in token ring acknowledgement is used or not.
But if still it comes anywhere then you can use the concept of piggybacking acknowledgement.
Means you can say yes, acknowledgement is there but piggybacking.
Piggybacking means what? You send acknowledgement along with the data so that
traffic is less. You will send acknowledgement separately, data separately,
better than this is what? You are sending data, send an acknowledgement along
so that packet transmission is less, the load or traffic on network will be less.
So that's why we use piggybacking. Then Differential Machester Coding is used.
And coding technique is multiple, NRZ, Differential Manchester, Manchester single,
so here we use Differential Machester Coding and what is Machester and Differential?
The link related to that you will get in recommended video
and you will get in description also. Because you should know that,
but in this video I will not explain it again, aready I have explained nicely.
Then we use Variable size framing here.
So like frame, we say, what nomenclature is used in Data Link Layer? Frame.
So when we send frames, do frame transmission, then frame can be static
means of same size or it can be of variable different sizes.
Then we have monitor. So here we use monitor station.
What is the purpose of monitor station? Many times what happens is,
like there is some packet, some frame, that frame is moving here
and that frame becomes corrupt, now because it is corrupt, no one is accepting it.
So that frame becomes a stray packet and it keeps moving, no one is accepting it.
So it is creating unnecessary traffic here, so what do we have to do?
We have to remove this type of packets. There are more things which we have to monitor.
So for that 1 station here works as a monitor. So these are the basic points
about token ring which can be asked in your competitive exams or college/university exams.
Then if we talk about frame format, here we use 2 frame formats, one for the data,
this is the frame format of data. And one is for token.
So here I am just giving you basic introduction but in further videos we will do this in detail
because it is difficult to cover everything in one video.
Then if we first talk about the data frame, in that comes Start Delimiter
which is of 1 byte, all these values are in bytes. Remember they are in bytes.
So Start Delimiter means packet starts from here, we use it for alerts,
we use it for syncronization. So this is information of 1 byte.
Then for Access Control, how to decide priority,
reserve bits are also in it, token is there or not... So we keep 1 byte of information here.
Then Frame Control, that your data, the packet you send, the frame that you send,
is that a data frame? Or control frame? Means may times we have to send control frames
rather than data, so that we know from here. Then this is Destination Address,
Source Address, 6 bytes each because we are working on MAC Address here.
So as you know MAC address is of 48 bits, 6 bytes you can say.
If we talk about the size of data then it depends, minimum obviously you can send of 0 bytes.
Maximum how much can you send? It depends on token holding time.
How long you can hold the token, on that depends how much maximum data you can send.
And this is an advantage, in Ethernet it is fixed, like 1500 bytes are fixed.
It cannot be more than that. But here you have an advantage as compared to Ethernet.
Then talking about CRC, we use Cyclic Redundancy Check for Error Control, CRC of 4 bytes.
Then here we have End Delimiter, like there is a start, similarly End Delimiter is of 1 byte.
And finally this is the frame status of 1 byte. I will tell you about this in detail.
Then talking about token, format of token is of 3 bytes, token is of 3 bytes
in which again Start Delimiter, Access Control and End Delimiter fields we use.
So we will discuss these in detail in the next video. Thank you.
